sentences.text: I can't look there.
We were between the very first firms who were nationalized.
Slovakia is an independent country, was a satellite of Germany, if not officially declared.
But they were completely under the control of German administration.
It was not visible German, but everywhere in the very important ministries there were always some Germans who'd keep an eye on them.
So I-- we-- our firm, because of the importance of economical and the size of it, was important firm.

And they started-- in 1939 they started to put the major firms-- it doesn't matter what sort of business-- they put, like, trustees of the government, which were known Jewish people, because most of the businesses in Slovakia, especially the big one and the medium-sized one, they're in Jewish hands.
There was very hardly a non-Jewish shop, let's say, in a small town.
There were-- there were only Jewish shops.
In a big city like Bratislava, which was the capital town, of course, there, I would say 1/3 were non-Jewish. 2/3 were Jewish.
Every leading manufacturer, or wholesaler-- the very important businesses-- were all in Jewish hands.
Now the government followed the dictatorship of Germany, and they want it to eliminate it, or change it as soon as possible.
Because in Germany, or in the protectorate-- in Prague and Moravia, which was converted and occupied by Germany-- Jews were sent out straightaway.
They were either sent to the concentration camps, or they were dismissed.
And only mostly German people took over the existing firms.
In Bratislava, they didn't take such severe methods, because the Slovaks didn't have enough able people to do that, first of all.
Not that they liked the Jews, so they left them there.
It was economically not possible.
So they named trustees in every business, in every Jewish firm.
The beginning started with the big firms, and we were in the first category of the larger firms.
So we were between the first dozen of leading shops in Bratislava who got trustees-- non-Jewish trustees.
They had a special name for it.
I can't translate it.
And these trustees were usually, or in many cases, women, which is unusual.
I can explain it now.

The big bosses, either in the new movement of Slovakian guards-- which was like SS-- there were in Germany, as you know, two militaristic powers-- the Wehrmacht, which was the regular army, and then there was the SS, which was the special army of Hitler, and the SS were also having influence in every country which they occupied, like in Czechoslovakia or Slovakia.
The local Germans formed immediately-- they couldn't form the army, because the army was officially Slovakian National Army.
But the second army was the SA-- the SA.
And they were National Socialist Army of Germans-- the German nationals, all right?
And they were the most dangerous ones.

So usually, these people who were-- who got suddenly the power, they were captains, and there were militaristic distinctions-- their wives got, as a reward, these very lucrative positions in the Jewish businesses.
We knew about it, because we were-- my brother, who was the manager of our firm, he was of the generation of the 34-- between 30 and 40 years of age.
And that the new crop of the new administrators-- the non-Jewish people.
But he went to school with almost any of them, you know?
And he knew the Minister of Justice, his colleague from the high school, or from the university.
And he had the very first-hand connections to these very influential people.
Through these connections, we were able to find out-- maybe one month ahead-- we will be nationalized.
We knew it.
And we arranged, again through these connections, then who should be the-- the trustee.
And we knew from the beginning, through the back door, that there will be two women named in trustee of our firm.
Now, we put there one woman, who was working for us for many years.

She came as a junior girl, she worked up to a leading position in the retail shop, so we pressed, and also with help of money, we arranged with the Minister of Commerce. that one-- this lady who will be our trustee should be so and so.
She was-- by the way, I have to disclose it, she was a girlfriend-- secret girlfriend-- of my brother, who was the manager-- still official manager of the firm.
The other woman, we didn't know.
We knew we will have to have two.
That was a precaution of the new fascist government.
When there are two strange women, one will watch the other.
They will-- they both governed.
They both steered quite nicely.
But that was the safety [INAUDIBLE].
And when we-- when we found out the other woman will be a strange person, so we arranged somehow-- no, we heard.
We heard, actually, before she came to the shop, she was named.
And she was the wife of a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] that was the local Slovakian equal to the SS, but the Slovakian part.
Because Slovakia had a proper army.
The second army was the Slovakian Guard, and above them was the German SS-- the local SS.
And this fellow who was a captain in the Slovakian Army, he was a very important personality.
And his wife-- he was-- he was a teacher officially, by profession.
He was a headmaster of a big school, which was two or three streets away from our business.
We knew him.
But we didn't know then his wife.
We found out only before she came.
We found out that the wife of this headmaster of the high school will be our second trustee.
This was very important, because we always tried to save ourselves-- not only personally, but the business-- as long as we can.
And through these connections to the governmental circles, we were able to manipulate this to a certain degree.
And really, this woman was named as a trustee.
And our girl, who was put there by us, she didn't want to.
She was a very religious person.
Mimi was her name, and she became, later on, my brother's-- my brother's wife.
So she was my sister-in-law, still alive in Slovakia now.
My brother died.
When she found out since that she will have another trustee as a partner in the big business, she was terribly afraid.
She didn't want to have it.
She wouldn't have, because she was afraid this woman will keep an eye on her, which really happened.
So she wasn't very keen, and we had to-- my father and my brother persuaded her.
Look, you-- you are doing it out of humanitarian feelings, and as a very believing Christian they-- they had such feelings.
Money was not important.
With money, she would have never taken that position.
So my father and my brother persuaded her, yes, just take it, you will see how it will go.
If there will be any danger for you, you can always resign, which was not possible.
Anyhow, it came to the fact that both women were named trustees of the firm.
On that day, when we got notification officially, my father was dismissed of being the owner and the managing director.
And my brother, with a lot of pushing, was able to be like a trustee in the firm, like helping the new managers.
It ?] was in 1939.
When the start of the Jewish persecution-- from '39 to '41-- we were able to juggle our survival.
Mind you, that-- that first transport from Slovakia started, in seriousness, in '41 and '42. '42 was already a very serious year.
They took, I remember, my wife's sister.
One day they declared-- we had a Jewish, like a head office for Jewish affairs, consisting of twelve Jewish prominent citizens.
That was the Judenrat, in German, all right?
And they-- whenever they had-- the Germans, whenever they wanted to do something against the Jews, they declared-- through the Jewish-- how would say 'rat'?
Jewish council.
Jewish Council.
Through the Jewish council, they introduced this and that, against Jewish laws, you know, in a nice, slow way.
So the Jewish council one day declared, tomorrow morning we need 1,000 young girls, starting from 16 years of age, and 1,000 young boys.
And they will go to Poland for work.
Please come tomorrow at seven o'clock, in such and such a place.
And it was all done with trickery, when the Jews are calling their own people-- have two pairs of boots, and have this and that blanket.
It sounded very, very real.

Then these young people are going for forced labor, because the propaganda of Germany was always that the Jews are not first to be equal, because they use the non-Jewish public, and they use swindles, and this and that.
And it would be time, or it is the time ripe then Jews should start to work hard for their living.
I come to that.
That happens from '39, because in '39 is, you know, Germany declared war against Russia-- against Poland.
And that was the beginning of the World War II.
It was very important.
And Czechoslovakia was already divided, from March until the war started-- about July, October-- against Poland.
And the tough anti-Jewish laws were introduced mainly from that time.

Gradually they built up to 1941, to such extents and in '42, were the first transports there, [? actually. ?] The very first transport was the young boys and young girls. 2,000 they took from Bratislava.
They were taking many more from their country, I guess.
I volunteered, that is the silly thing.
In 1939, when I came home-- in '38 and '39 I was already home-- the-- I was called to the army as a active service, because we had two years active service to go to the army.
But my two brothers, the older one and the middle one were already in the army.
They were called to the two years active service.
And there is an unwritten law everywhere, in every country, if a family has two children, two sons in the army, the third one does not need to go.
But you have to ask for it.
So I didn't ask, and my parents had lots of other worries.
They didn't worry.
One day, I-- my year was called up, and I went.
And I went with pleasure, because I wanted to defend my country, despite the fact that it was a fascist country, but we were still a free republic.
When the war was declared, that was the start of the German war against the whole East.
So I was called to the army.
We got uniforms like any other new recruit.
But the second day, they called all the Jewish boys in the-- I came to the army in Bratislava, which was the capital town of the country.
And when I entered the army barracks with maybe about 30 other Jewish boys-- there were not many-- we were suddenly exchanged, the uniforms.
The captain, or whoever was in charge, they gave us shabby, dirty uniforms.
They said, from now on you are just bloody Jews.
And you will be in the army, but as a working-- as a working unit.
We didn't even know what was going to happen.
When I was-- I was in the army for about three weeks in Bratislava, in our hometown, and then they shipped us to the mountains.
We have got a big range of mountains on the border, from Poland and Slovakia, that are equal to Alps.
Tatra.
They are Tatra mountains.
And that is almost the whole area between Slovakia and Poland.
Had a natural barrier between the two countries, because these high mountains-- like Alps in Germany or in Austria-- you can't just cross it or what is in Spain, the Pyrenees, something of that sort.
So we were sent the first 200-- they collected other Jewish soldiers from other [INAUDIBLE] zones in the area of Bratislava, and about 200 of us were shipped by train to the border of Poland.
That was about three weeks before the Polish war started.
But Slovakian fascist government, they knew already.
And that was definitely the influence of Germany, when they said, we don't want any Jewish soldiers, because my two brothers were officers in the army.
One of them was in the army about six months, and he was in charge of a small garrison, because they didn't have enough.
They didn't have officer or Sergeant-Majors.
And whoever-- and every Jewish soldier was maybe the best educated and most able.
So I-- my father, when they found out I have to-- when I wanted to go to the military service, was terribly angry.
And he could have got me out, but I said no.
If you do anything against it, I will volunteer on my own, because that was still-- we were still part of a Slovakian republic, which was nothing to do-- officially nothing to do-- with Germany.
But we didn't know that.
So I was shipped with the other soldiers to the border of Poland and Slovakia.
And we were-- we were then altogether about 400, maybe 500 Jewish boys collected from all the territory of Slovakia.
And we were sent there to dig trenches on the mountains.
And we were to the neck in snow.
After three weeks doing really-- they treated us like-- we never knew such a treatment before-- like prisoners.
We were-- we were down under the-- under the slopes of the mountains.
It was a big military garrison.
And the Jewish soldiers were all concentrated there.
There were also another group, other barracks.
We were in two or three barracks place.
That other barracks had non-Jewish soldiers, and they treated us-- it was the first time how the Jews are treated.
Still, when I think back now, they were treating us, still, with gloves, the Slovakian soldiers.
They used slur words-- you dirty Jew, and so on.
But we were not smacked.
We were not kicked.
We were only [NON-ENGLISH], which means we were-- we were treated purposely badly.

Like when we worked the whole day, digging the trenches on the mountain slopes, and we came home, because that was 5 or 10 degrees below 0, so very, very severe conditions, we came home hungry, and exhausted.
So we have to wait on the yard, and the-- the officer in charge said, all right, now we will show you how you have to be trained.
And we had to run around the yard in the garrison-- down, up, down, up.
Purposely, we were shown that they are the masters.
And when we were half dead, then they said, OK, now you go to the barracks and have your black coffee with a piece of bread or something.
So it was a hardship.
After three weeks of being treated like that, I-- I wasn't very strong fellow, never.
I was a skinny child, actually.
And I got a terrible cold one day.
And they sent-- they marched us from the village where was our headquarter.
We had to march to our working place on the slopes of the mountains and different places.
And after being-- I had a very severe cold.
I didn't feel well enough, and I said to the commander, whoever, that I want to go to the revere, to the doctor.
He said, you d-- Jew, you just go.
You don't need anything.
And he said, you can have two aspirins.
So I was taking two aspirins.
Happened the second day.
The third day, I was so sick that I collapsed in the working trenches, where we were working.
And I was the transported to a military hospital.
I was-- when they took me from the working place, I was really unconscious.
They had to carry me somewhere.
I found myself in the military hospital, which was about 80 kilometers from the place where our unit was stationed.
And that was-- that was already when the war broke out again Poland.
This military hospital was in a big town, which was Ruzomberok.
The name of the place was Ruzomberok.
There was a big military strong point.
And because of the military hospital, it was an important place.
When they placed me in the military hospital, you know, it's different than if you say hospital.
It's completely a different attitude to the military.
In military hospital, you are also a number.
You are nobody.
You have to be under the command of the-- of the fellow who's in charge of the-- of the group.
There were about 30 soldiers in the group.
And they couldn't care less.
And then they-- when they knew, that fellow is a Jew, they didn't visibly or personally attack me.
But they treated me like a Jew.
So I became very sick, and I got tonsillitis.
My tonsils were swollen, and they had to take them out.
So when they-- when I was told tomorrow we will operate.
We have to take out your tonsils, I was so weak I couldn't care less.
I had a high fever, and whatever they gave me, or told me, I had to do.
So I had the walk to the operating room, and the fellow who was operating on me was a doctor.
And he called two strong soldiers.
He said, you sit in the chair.
He put me in the chair.
And the two soldiers, which he called in there, they were on the-- on the corridor like guards.
He said, you hold him by his shoulders.
If he moves, you get three days in the brig.
So you can imagine, they hold me.
And he while he was-- he gave me injection.
But in the middle of the operation, he broke the instrument which he scraped my throat.
And only thanks to the nurse who was there, a woman, she encouraged him.
He said, I can't do it, I can't do it.
He was panicking.
She said, but you have to finish.
The fellow is bleeding to death.
You have to do it.
Do it This way, that way.
She gave him help.
And so he finally finished somehow, and when I was ready, I was half dead.
And they said, now you march down, because we were already treated like Jews.
Before that, it would have never happened, you know?
So they marched me down, and I was 1 and 1/2 months recovering.
I got inflammation of lung and the tonsils.
So I didn't have a very pleasant time.
But luckily, the news-- I was not able to write, or to let my people know where I am, and what happens to me.
But somehow, I mentioned it to a-- I don't know if he was a Jewish-- he must've been a Jewish soldier, another one.
And he somehow notified my family, and my brother, who was nearest to the place where I was in the hospital, he came there.
And he was in uniform.
He was not treated like an ordinary.
We were already treated like the members of a working-- like a concentration camp.
So he came there, and because of his look, and his connections, he found what are the doctors.
And he bribed one of them to give me in the evening-- because there was no penicillin, but there was a German substitute of penicillin-cibazol.
And he bribed him, to give me in the night, two pills, or four pills.
Whatever was necessary in secret, because they wouldn't give it to the-- to the Jews.
I have to mention at this stage, then they brought in the first injured soldiers from the front, because the front was about 60 or 80 kilometers from the military hospital.
When they-- when the-- there were German injured soldiers, and also captured Polish.
But when the Polish soldiers, which were captured, when they were injured, they had to give-- they had to bring them to the hospital.
That was the big military hospital.
So they put two people in one bed, because there was such a shortage.
Luckily, they didn't put anyone to me, because they said, ah, maybe he will die anyhow.
And so I was not-- I was not bothered.
And there I was, only until my brother started to arrange special treatment, because in the book, I found out after, every military patient has a diary, and they write so and so.
And in my diary, on my name was written, very small chance of survival.
Patient will most probably not survive.
That was the-- but thank God my brother had so much influence, that he put me through.
And when I was almost-- almost two months, when I was ready and commanded to go back to my unit, the unit, luckily, was replaced, not from the mountains, which we were before.
It was commanded to go down south about in the middle of Slovakia.
For what reason, I don't know.
Maybe we were too close to the war zone.
So I'm sure the Germans must have influenced the decision of the high command of the Slovakian army.
Anyhow, I was sent to the new place, new unit, which was already much better.
But I was so weak and so unable to do even the ordinary things which the soldiers were have to-- train in the yard or do some work.
So through connection of my brother and my father, who was still influential, they got me out.
That was in year '40-- '39, yes, about '40.
So they got me out on account that I was physically so run down that I'm not able to continue my military service, and, I am sure, with bribery.
I was released, and I never returned.
I went back to Bratislava, where we have still our home.
I had to recover.
I don't know how long the recovery took.
And I was working on and off.
Not officially-- officially, I couldn't be there.
So, more or less, I was half-official.
If they would have been looking for me, I'm sure my parents would have arranged to hide me somehow.
Maybe the military service, because I was not released officially.
I was only given a long holiday, something like that.
I-- I couldn't even follow what happened to me.
I was so run down.
And that went on from '41 till '42.
So I was working on and off, with the Father's firm, but gradually, more and more anti-Jewish laws were introduced.
And the life became more and more miserable.
The laws were introduced that we couldn't have non-Jewish helpers.
A Jew couldn't own any business.
A Jew couldn't go out after six o'clock in the evening.
They took-- first of all, they took away the radios.
And that was very important, because during the war, everyone listened to the radio.
That gave you the clue what will happen tomorrow.
So radios were confiscated.
Jewelry was confiscated.
Only the wedding ring, man or woman, could keep.
And these were the Nuremberg laws, the German originals, but they didn't introduce them all of a sudden, but gradually.
The trustee.
My father was dismissed, as I've mentioned before.
He was dismissed.
That means he was living-- we were living in one of the houses where we had our main shop.
That-- in Europe, there are houses, which are in Vienna and Bratislava, 300 years old.
And they have huge, beautiful buildings.
You have got [INAUDIBLE] which is like the whole ground floor here.
And we were  a wealthy family, so we were living-- luckily, we were left in our place.
Whoever, whichever Jewish shop was-- there were a lot of Jewish shops, smaller ones.
They were closed.
They didn't nationalize everyone.
They said, this shop is unnecessary.
They close.
And the Jews were dislocated.
They were called the dislocation of Jews.
They sent them from Bratislava, which was the capital town.
They sent them somewhere to the country.
And the man, if he was able to work, they said, oh, you will work in the coal mine.
Or you will work on the agricultural place, such and such.
No questions asked, have you family?
They picked them up, and shipped them to 200, 300 kilometers away.
That was the first physical hardship which they introduced.
Of course, there were always people-- like our family, was very well known.
And we knew so many prominent Slovaks-- Ministers and the big shots in the government.
So we were always able to get some sort of dispensation.
I don't know if you ever heard, from 1941, the Jewish people were issued with a white paper.
Then, a year after, a yellow paper.
And then they were the biggest paper, which gave you almost freedom, was the presidential exemption.
Presentation exemptions were issued maybe to 100 Jews, altogether.
And they were the people who got from the president of the country a special decree that Jewish paragraphs do not apply to this and this person.
And that was something which was more than money.
Not everybody could get it.
We knew we couldn't get it.
Because there were Jews who were working in the government for many years.
Or the-- especially many of these dispensations were given to Jews with mixed marriages.
So we couldn't get everything.
But we were able to stick to our-- not me, but my brother who was still officially employed-- and we were able to live in our flat, which was very big plus.
And we-- we-- tried to survive, from one week, from one month to the other.
What did you do for money?
Money, we had-- every Jew was well-- not every, but most of them-- were well off.
And if they didn't change the money to some other currency, that was all right.
They did it later on.
The money, we got from our savings and the [INAUDIBLE] the trustee woman, which we had.
She was so honest.
Then she took, I don't know, 10,000 crown wages, and she always said, I don't need this money.
And she gave it-- she tried to give it to my father.
My father said no, because we knew as long as she will be well-fed.
But she did it out of humanity and out of very good relationship with my brother.
How she-- how-- she's all right, this Gentile.
She saved his life later.
That's a different story.
So we got the money.
That was not our worry.
So when did things start to get really bad, that your lives were at risk?
From 1942.
From 1942, they started to ship the first shipments-- I call it shipments-- the transports to Poland, because Auschwitz-- what is it called in English?
Auschwitz--
Auschwitz.
You see how you have a [? blackout? ?] Auschwitz was in-- under construction.
We knew it was not ready.
These 1,000 young men and 1,000 young boys-- women, they were the ones who went to Auschwitz to build the physical start of the Auschwitz.
One person, who we know here from this group of 1,000 girls is still alive and lives here.
I know her because she comes from Bratislava.
The other girl who was taken, was the sister of my wife.
She was one year older.
She didn't return.
Nobody returned, only the one which I know here returned, because she was put, right from the beginning in Auschwitz, in an office.
She spoke four languages fluently-- German fluently, Hungarian, Slovak.
So the Germans needed-- for establishing the administration, they needed such people.
And she was lucky that she got that job.
And she survived.
Usually, from these 2,000 people, almost nobody survived.
When the transportation started.
From 1942, when we already knew they-- they took-- tomorrow night-- we-- we got our spies in the official administration.
And we knew a day before, sometimes two days before, don't sleep home, because they are coming to collect Jews.
So we were always-- we had a very big circle of non-Jewish friends, who were willing and able to either shelter my sister or my brother, or-- my father and my mother was the hardest.
And they usually didn't even go.
They said, no, we can't, because each parent wanted to save the children.
That was the priority.
So many times, when we really ran out from our home and went to different places to hide, my parents didn't go.
And one of the such nights was tragic, then they took my father.
That was in 1943, '44.
He died.
And we don't know in Auschwitz, in one of the big concentration camps.
He died.
He didn't return.
From all the family, we all returned, so we were extremely lucky.
When they-- when they came, that was in '44.
When they came to take my family, because the SS-- and the local fascists, the Slovak fascists-- they came to every Jewish house.
You see, our-- the Jewish council had full addresses from every Jew who was still living in Bratislava.
And when the police, or the secret police, made the phone call-- we need at least 1,000 people.
Give us the list tomorrow or tonight.
They did it.
They had to do it.
So they knew who is at home, and who is not.
They knew about our families.
But we had always some sort of connections to be warned.
And when we were warned, the children went in hiding.
My parents didn't want to go.
They said, we are too old.
We don't want to go.
And on many occasions, luckily they didn't come to our house.
But on one of these occasions, unfortunately, they found my father and my mother at home.
And my father-- we had-- we had a very big country-- like, a townhouse, a big one.
And the entrance was like here, but with gates.
No, the entrance was like in a passage, because in the front was one of our shops, one of our businesses.
So there was a side entrance to the building.
In the side entrance to the actual flat, we had lot of staircase, like winding staircase.
It was a baroque style house.
And to make it safe, when these tragic times started, my father installed a steel gate, which is, like, shutters, you know, which you-- like harmonica shutters, if you know what I mean.
During the day, it was open.
For the night, they always locked it, and put even the lock there.
So when they came that particular night, I don't even know which day or which month it was.
They came and wanted to collect us and the parents.
My father was already up.
It was two o'clock, I think, in the morning.
They banged on the steel gate, open up, open up.
And my father pushed my mother.
We had on the top floor, not a secret passage, like a little staircase, inside staircase, leading to the roof, you know?
And my father forced my mother to-- she was only in the nightie, dressing gown-- or she put the dressing gown on.
He said, you go.
Go upstairs and hide anywhere.
Hide behind, because we had huge chimneys there.
And the roof, was like, so big, like this room.
Every section of it was like a separate room.
We used to dry clothes in there.
